mnemonics.title: DNS Resolution Order
mnemonics.content: B.H.D.N.S - Browser cache → Hosts file → DNS cache → DNS server → Server query. Remember: 'Big Horses Don't Need Shoes'

mnemonics.title: HTTP Status Codes
mnemonics.content: 1xx: Informational - 'I'nformation, 2xx: Success - 'S'uccess, 3xx: Redirection - 'R'edirect, 4xx: Client Error - 'C'lient Error, 5xx: Server Error - 'S'erver Error. Remember: 'I'See Red Cats Scream'

mnemonics.title: HTTP Methods CRUD
mnemonics.content: GET (Read), POST (Create), PUT (Update/Replace), DELETE (Delete), PATCH (Update/Modify). Remember: 'GPPDP' or 'Giant Pandas Prefer Delicious Plants'

mnemonics.title: DNS Record Types
mnemonics.content: A (Address), AAAA (Address IPv6), CNAME (Canonical Name), MX (Mail Exchange), SOA (Start of Authority), NS (Name Server). Remember: 'A AC M S N' = 'Auntie's Apple Cake Makes Sweet Noodles'

mnemonics.title: HTTP Request Structure
mnemonics.content: Method + URI + Version, Headers, Body. Remember: 'MVB' = Method Version Body

acronyms.usage: Translates domain names (like google.com) into IP addresses that computers use to identify each other
Protocol used for transferring web pages and web resources between client and server
The address of a web resource, includes protocol, domain, path, and optional parameters
General identifier for web resources; URL is a type of URI
Application layer protocol that automatically assigns IP addresses to devices on a network
Protocol for sending emails between mail servers
Protocol for transferring files between client and server
In DNS, specifies how long a resolver should cache a query result before requesting it again

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a giant phone book (DNS server) where you look up 'John's number' (domain name) and find his phone number (IP address). The lookup travels from your phone (client) → local phone book (cache) → directory assistance (recursive DNS) → main database (root/TLD/authoritative servers)

Picture a restaurant: You (client) give your order (HTTP request) to the waiter (HTTP), the kitchen (server) prepares food (processes request), and the waiter brings back your meal (HTTP response) with a bill (status code)

Visualize an inverted tree: Root (.) at the top → TLD servers (.com, .edu) in the middle → Authoritative servers at bottom. Like a corporate hierarchy: CEO (root) → Department heads (TLD) → Managers (Authoritative)

Think of a 4-story building (TCP/IP): Application layer (top floor), Transport (stairs), Internet (elevator), Link (ground floor). OSI is the same building with more detailed room divisions (7 floors)

analogies.analogy: DNS is like a city's telephone directory or a mall's directory. Just as you look up a store name in a mall directory to find its floor and unit number, DNS looks up a website name to find its IP address (its 'phone number' on the internet)

HTTP is like a restaurant's ordering system. The menu lists available items (GET requests), you can place new orders (POST), modify existing orders (PUT/PATCH), or cancel orders (DELETE). The chef's response tells you if your order was successful (200 OK) or if there's a problem (404 Not Found)

DNS cache is like your brain's memory of frequently dialed numbers. Instead of looking up a contact every time, your phone remembers recently called numbers. This speeds up repeated visits to the same websites

HTTP cookies are like a restaurant's loyalty card. The server gives you a numbered card (cookie) on your first visit. On future visits, you present the card, and the restaurant (server) recognizes you and recalls your previous preferences/orders
HTTP is like sending a postcard - anyone can read the message as it travels. HTTPS is like sending a sealed, encrypted letter in a secure envelope - only the intended recipient can read it

Recursive query is like asking a librarian to find a book and bring it to you (they do all the work). Iterative query is like asking the librarian where to look, then you go there, ask again, and keep searching yourself until you find it
